title: How to Prep for a Hike
author: Mountain Slayers Kenya
excerpt: Spring's vibrant energy stirs the soul, whispering tales of adventure and exploration. A well-planned expedition is the key to transforming your hiking aspirations into a cherished memory.
content:
Spring's vibrant energy stirs the soul, whispering tales of adventure and exploration. Perhaps a breathtaking mountain peak beckons you, or the quietude of a forest path calls your name. Whatever your hiking aspirations may be, a well-planned expedition is the key to transforming them from wishful thinking into a cherished memory. Here's a comprehensive guide to ensure you conquer the trails with confidence and transform your hike into an unforgettable experience.

First things first, delve into the exciting world of trail options! Lace up your metaphorical research boots and scout for paths that align perfectly with your skillset and desires. Reviews from fellow hikers can be invaluable, offering insights into the trail's difficulty, distance, elevation changes, and hidden gems you might miss otherwise. Don't be afraid to challenge yourself, but also prioritize your comfort and safety. Remember, the most rewarding hikes are the ones you thoroughly enjoy.

With your chosen path laid out before you, peek into the future with a weather forecast. Mother Nature can be a fickle companion, so be prepared for anything she throws your way. Whether it's scorching sunshine or a sudden downpour, packing accordingly will ensure you stay comfortable and safe throughout your journey.

Safety should always be your top priority. Don't embark on your solo adventure without letting a friend or family member know your plans. Share the trail you've chosen, your estimated return time, and any other pertinent details. This simple step could make all the difference in case of an unforeseen situation.

Now, let's get down to the nitty-gritty: your gear. Picture yourself gliding effortlessly along the trail, a backpack comfortably nestled on your shoulders. Within its depths lie the essentials for a successful hike: a life-sustaining supply of water (more than you think you'll need!), a bounty of energy-boosting snacks, sunscreen to shield you from the sun's rays, and a first-aid kit to address any minor bumps or scrapes.

Clothing is your armor against the elements. Embrace the wisdom of layering. Start with a breathable base layer made from wool or polyester, a cozy insulating layer like fleece for warmth, and finally, a waterproof outer shell to protect you from the rain. Don't underestimate the power of comfortable footwear. Invest in well-fitting, broken-in hiking boots or shoes that provide ample ankle support and superior traction. Blisters and twisted ankles are no fun companions on a hike!

Think beyond the basics! Consider packing a map and compass (or a GPS device with a downloaded map) as a failsafe navigation system in case you veer off course. A headlamp or flashlight tucked away in your pack can be a lifesaver if your hike extends into the twilight hours. Every little bit counts when it comes to ensuring a smooth and enjoyable adventure.

Finally, prepare your body for the glorious challenge ahead. Even seemingly easy hikes can test your physical limits. Embark on shorter walks or hikes with increasing difficulty in the weeks leading up to your main adventure. This will help you build endurance and prevent muscle fatigue from turning your hike into a struggle.

Hydration is paramount. In the days leading up to your hike, make water your best friend. Dehydration can sneak up on you quickly on the trail, sapping your energy and making the experience far less pleasurable. Fuel your body with a nutritious breakfast on the day of your hike, and don't forget to pack plenty of healthy snacks to keep your energy levels soaring throughout the journey.

By following these comprehensive steps, you'll transform yourself from an armchair adventurer into a confident trailblazer. So lace up your boots, grab your backpack, and get ready to embark on an unforgettable exploration of the great outdoors!

title: Top 10 Kenya Hiking Trails
author: Mountain Slayers Kenya
excerpt: From the beginner-friendly Ngong Hills to the challenging peaks of the Aberdares — here are the best hiking trails near Nairobi for every level.
content:
BEGINNER-FRIENDLY TRAILS

#1 William Hill – A child-friendly option for hiking near Nairobi
Forming a part of the Kikuyu escarpment, William Hill is located about 75 Km North West of Nairobi. The hill stands at approximately 2300m above sea level and makes for a moderate, beginner-friendly, child-friendly hiking trail near Nairobi. Most of the trail is on a relatively flat dirt road, with only a small, steep rocky scramble up the escarpment as the hike ends.

The William Hill hiking trail traditionally starts at the old Italian Church along the Mai Mahiu highway, with an initial 10km trek along a dusty murram road, before taking a steep 3km ascent up the Kikuyu escarpment. The climb, though slightly steep, is rewarding with sweeping views of the Great Rift Valley, Mt. Longonot, Kijabe Forest and Hills, and Mt. Margaret.

#2 Ngong Hills — The default choice for most Nairobi hiking groups
Another popular Kenya hiking trail near Nairobi, Ngong Hills, is located approximately 30km from the capital. With a gradual incline and mostly grassland terrain, it is a relatively easy hike and a suitable choice for beginners — and virtually anyone looking for a gentle walk.

Offering panoramic views of the city and the surrounding mountain ranges, the trail has several incredible viewpoints. The hike up Ngong Hills starts at the northern foot of the hills and traverses the whole range to the southern end at "Kona Baridi." The hike covering all seven hills takes 4–6 hours, depending on one's pace and fitness level. A windproof jacket is a must-have, as the Ngong Hills is notorious for cold, strong winds.

#3 Kilimambogo — One of the best family hiking trails near Nairobi
Located off the Thika-Garissa Highway in the Oldonyo Sabuk National Park and rising to an elevation of 2,145m above sea level, the Kilimambogo hike is an excellent choice for any beginner hiker looking for a gentle climb. Two major paths can be used: a 9km open road that winds up the mountain, or a 6km footpath that cuts through the forest. The hike to the summit takes approximately two to three hours.

MODERATE TRAILS

#4 Kijabe Hills — A moderate one-way trail
The Kijabe Hills are located North West of Nairobi and are part of the Kikuyu Escarpment. The most popular trail starts at the AIC Kijabe Hospital, walking along the railway line before cutting through thick forest to the caves and hot springs, then ascending steeply to a maximum elevation of 2,688m above sea level.

#5 Mt. Longonot — A favorite Kenya hiking trail
Rising to 2,776 meters above sea level, this extinct volcano in the Great Rift Valley is a popular spot for Nairobi hiking groups. The 3.1km trail up to the rim takes 45 minutes to two hours. Once at the edge, you can descend or add 7.2km by going around the crater rim, which takes two to three hours. The entire hike averages 4–6 hours.

CHALLENGING TRAILS

#6 Rurimeria — The badass Kenya hiking trail everyone is talking about
Rising to 3,860m above sea level, Rurimeria is the third-highest peak of the Aberdare Range and one of the most challenging day hiking trails near Nairobi. It features 4 distinct ridges (three false summits), with the first being the toughest. Waterproof boots and good rain gear are essential.

#7 Elephant Hill — The notorious Aberdare brat
The 9km Elephant Hill trail starts at the Njabini Forest Station gate and ends at the summit at 3,658m above sea level. The trail passes through planted forest, bamboo forest, and past the infamous "point of despair." At a moderate pace, the hike takes 7–10 hours.

#8 Table Mountain — A short, sweet affair
The fourth-highest peak in the Aberdare Range at 3,792m, Table Mountain is known for its maddening steepness. The 6.2km trail from Mikeu involves a near-vertical ascent, a river crossing, and a final traverse through moorland. Waterproof boots are a must in the rainy season.

#9 Seven Ponds — The one trail you will love and hate in equal measure!
Located between Table Mountain and Rurimeria, the Seven Ponds trail is approximately 5.5km each way and ends at 3,831m above sea level at seven natural ponds in the northern Nyandarua moorland. Ideal for altitude and endurance training for high-altitude hikes like Mt. Kenya and Mt. Kilimanjaro.

#10 Mt. Kinangop — The mother of them all
Rising to 3,906m above sea level, Mt. Kinangop is the second-highest peak in the Aberdare Range. It covers 14km each way and takes 7–10 hours on average, though some slower hikers may take up to 14 hours. Notoriously strenuous — not for the faint-hearted.

title: Why Mountain Slayers: SLAYERS – KENYA
author: Mountain Slayers Kenya
excerpt: As we begin our monthly hikes of 2017, it is important to expound on the reason why Mountain Slayers exists and what its primary objective is. Our single vision is: 'To unwrap the world, one destination at a time.'
content:
As we begin our monthly hikes of 2017, it is important to expound on the reason why Mountain Slayers exists and what its primary objective is. Our single vision is:

'To unwrap the world, one destination at a time'

The world is God's gift to us. Mountain Slayers seeks to unwrap this gift through exploring various destinations in Kenya, East Africa and the world at large. There is no better way to interact with the world than on foot.

Our tenet as Mountain Slayers – Kenya is: 'Treasure and nourish relationships'

• Relationship with God
• Relationship with Self
• Relationship with others
• Relationship with the environment

In line with these four areas we create time to be in the great outdoors, enjoying God's creation, using the quiet time in nature for introspection, using the moments on expeditions to encourage or receive encouragement from fellow slayers, and finally appreciate the healing qualities of the different environments we explore.

Each hike comes with its unique challenges and we as slayers have learnt that by taking 'One step at a time' we are able to get to the summit and conquer whatever lies on our path.

To get the most out of the hikes, Mountain Slayers adhere to the following values:

• Integrity — Being authentic, honest and morally upright as an individual
• Respect — Of yourself and the team
• Commitment — To yourself, the team and the process
• Non-judgmental — Accepting of each other as unique individuals

We hope that everyone who chooses to slay with us this year will enjoy and grow in their journey with us.

Here is to a fun-filled slaying 2017!!

title: Train For Mt Kenya: A Beginner's Guide
author: Mountain Slayers Kenya
excerpt: Embarking on a journey to climb Mt. Kenya is not just about reaching its majestic summit — it's a test of physical endurance, mental resilience, and sheer determination. Here's how to prepare.
content:
INTRODUCTION

Embarking on a journey to climb Mt. Kenya is not just about reaching its majestic summit; it's a test of physical endurance, mental resilience, and sheer determination. As one of Africa's highest peaks, standing proudly at over 5,000 meters above sea level, Mt. Kenya offers an exhilarating challenge to adventurers from around the world. However, to conquer this formidable mountain, adequate preparation through training is paramount.

UNDERSTANDING THE CHALLENGE

Before delving into training specifics, it's crucial to understand the unique challenges posed by Mt. Kenya. Unlike its neighbor Kilimanjaro, Mt. Kenya's terrain is more technical and requires a certain level of fitness and skill. Hikers can expect steep ascents, rocky trails, and potentially unpredictable weather conditions, including cold temperatures and occasional snowfall. Preparing both physically and mentally is key to a successful summit attempt.

BUILDING ENDURANCE

Building endurance is perhaps the most critical aspect of training for the Mt. Kenya hike. Ascent to the summit often involves long hours of trekking, sometimes spanning multiple days. Incorporate activities such as hiking, running, cycling, and stair climbing into your training regimen. Focus on gradually increasing the duration and intensity of your workouts to improve cardiovascular fitness and stamina.

STRENGTH TRAINING

In addition to endurance, strength training plays a vital role in preparing your body for the physical demands of hiking Mt. Kenya. Target major muscle groups — legs, core, and upper body — through exercises such as squats, lunges, planks, push-ups, and pull-ups. Strengthening these muscle groups will enhance your hiking performance and reduce the risk of injury, especially when navigating challenging terrain.

ALTITUDE ACCLIMATIZATION

One of the most significant challenges of climbing Mt. Kenya is dealing with high altitude. As you ascend, the air becomes thinner, making it harder for your body to obtain the oxygen it needs. To mitigate the effects of altitude sickness, consider incorporating altitude training into your regimen if possible — through simulated altitude workouts or gradual exposure to higher elevations during practice hikes.

PRACTICE HIKES

There's no better way to prepare for hiking Mt. Kenya than by embarking on practice hikes in similar terrain. Seek out local trails with varying elevation gains and challenging terrain to simulate the conditions you'll encounter on the mountain. This helps build physical fitness, lets you test your gear, and allows you to gauge your overall readiness.

NUTRITION AND HYDRATION

Proper nutrition and hydration are crucial for sustaining energy levels and optimizing performance. Prioritize a well-balanced diet rich in carbohydrates, protein, healthy fats, and plenty of fruits and vegetables. Stay hydrated by drinking ample water throughout the day, especially during training sessions and high-altitude hikes.

REST AND RECOVERY

Lastly, don't underestimate the importance of rest and recovery in your training plan. Adequate sleep and rest days are essential for muscle repair, injury prevention, and overall well-being. Listen to your body, and don't hesitate to adjust your training schedule or intensity if you're feeling fatigued or experiencing any discomfort.
